\title{\textbf{TEI-Bench: A Controlled, Held-Out Evaluation of the\\
Target--Evaluate--Improve Loop Across 31 Agentic Tasks}}

\author[1]{Orkhan Javadli}
\author[2]{Anni Zimina}
\affil[1]{Massachusetts Institute of Technology}
\affil[2]{Stanford University}
\date{\today}

\begin{document}
\maketitle

\begin{abstract}
Evaluation-guided, automated prompt optimization promises to improve agentic
large-language-model (LLM) systems without manual prompt engineering, yet most
supporting evidence is anecdotal: single tasks, in-sample evaluation, and
subjective LLM-as-judge metrics scored by a model from the same family as the
system under test. We present \textbf{TEI-Bench}, a controlled study of the
\emph{Target--Evaluate--Improve} (TEI) loop --- a GPA-style multi-dimensional
evaluator~\cite{gpa2025} coupled to a GEPA-style reflective, Pareto-based
prompt optimizer~\cite{gepa2025} --- across \textbf{\nAgents{} agents spanning
\nAgents{} industries}. We adopt three safeguards absent from prior
demonstrations: (i) a strict \emph{train/test split} so all reported scores are
on held-out inputs; (ii) an \emph{objective primary endpoint} (accuracy /
exact match) computed in code, with the four GPA dimensions as secondary; and
(iii) \emph{judge--agent separation}, optimizing and judging a weaker model
(\texttt{claude-haiku-4-5}) with a stronger, different model
(\texttt{claude-sonnet-4-5}). On held-out test data, TEI raises the objective
score from \objBase{} to \objAfter{} (mean $\Delta=\objDelta$, $95\%$ CI
$[\objCILow,\objCIHigh]$, paired $t=\objT$, $p=\objP$, Cohen's $d_z=\objDz$;
\objWins{}/\objLosses{}/\objTies{} win/loss/tie), and the GPA aggregate from
\gpaBase{} to \gpaAfter{} ($\Delta=\gpaDelta$, $p=\gpaP$). Improvements
concentrate on tasks with measurable headroom and on the dimensions a prompt
can actually control (execution accuracy and target alignment), while reasoning
soundness is essentially unchanged --- a pattern consistent with the mechanism
of prompt optimization. We release all code, the \nAgents{} task datasets,
every agent output trace, and all optimized prompts.
\end{abstract}

\section{Introduction}
LLM agents are increasingly deployed in production, yet the practice of
\emph{improving} them remains manual and ad hoc. The Target--Evaluate--Improve
(TEI) loop operationalizes a closed feedback cycle: \emph{target} the
objective, \emph{evaluate} the agent on structured dimensions, and
\emph{improve} the system --- here, its prompt --- automatically. The approach
combines two recent lines of work: multi-dimensional agent evaluation in the
spirit of GPA~\cite{gpa2025}, and reflective, Pareto-based prompt optimization
as in GEPA~\cite{gepa2025}, building on the broader program-optimization view
of DSPy~\cite{dspy2023,mipro2024}.

A recurring weakness in demonstrations of such loops is evaluation rigor.
Reported gains are often (a) measured on the same examples used to optimize,
(b) on a single task, and (c) scored by an LLM judge from the same family as
the optimized system, inviting self-preference bias~\cite{llmjudge2023}. These
choices inflate apparent gains and make it impossible to tell whether the loop
\emph{generalizes}. This paper asks a deliberately conservative question:
\emph{does the benefit of an evaluation-guided prompt-optimization loop survive
held-out evaluation, an objective metric, and judge--agent separation, when
measured across many diverse tasks?}

\paragraph{Contributions.}
\begin{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{TEI-Bench}: a suite of \nAgents{} agentic classification and
  reasoning tasks across \nAgents{} industries, with committed train/test
  splits --- public datasets where available, and model-generated,
  model-validated data otherwise.
  \item A \textbf{controlled paired protocol} with an objective primary
  endpoint, held-out evaluation, and judge--agent model separation.
  \item \textbf{Statistically powered results} ($n=\nAgents$): paired
  $t$-test, Wilcoxon signed-rank, Cohen's $d_z$, and bootstrap confidence
  intervals, with a pre-registered headroom-subset analysis.
  \item A \textbf{full open release} of code, data, per-example traces, and
  optimized prompts for exact reproduction.
\end{enumerate}

\section{Related Work}
\paragraph{Automated prompt optimization.}
APE~\cite{ape2022} and OPRO~\cite{opro2023} frame prompt search as
LLM-driven optimization; DSPy~\cite{dspy2023} and MIPROv2~\cite{mipro2024} cast
multi-stage LLM programs as optimizable objects and jointly tune instructions
and demonstrations; TextGrad~\cite{textgrad2024} backpropagates natural-language
``gradients.'' GEPA~\cite{gepa2025} introduces reflective, Pareto-based prompt
evolution that diagnoses failures in natural language and merges complementary
candidates, often matching or exceeding reinforcement learning at a fraction of
the rollouts. TEI uses a GEPA-style reflective Pareto optimizer as its
``Improve'' stage.
\paragraph{Self-improvement and reflection.}
Self-Refine~\cite{selfrefine2023} and Reflexion~\cite{reflexion2023} show that
LLMs can improve via self-generated feedback. TEI differs by separating the
\emph{evaluator} from the \emph{system under test} and by optimizing a reusable
prompt rather than a single trajectory.
\paragraph{Agent evaluation.}
LLM-as-judge protocols~\cite{llmjudge2023} are now standard but carry
self-preference and verbosity biases. GPA~\cite{gpa2025} decomposes agent
quality into goal/plan/action-aligned dimensions. TEI uses a GPA-style
four-dimension judge as a \emph{secondary} endpoint and pairs it with a
code-computed objective \emph{primary} endpoint to neutralize judge bias.

\section{The TEI Loop}
\paragraph{Target.} Each agent is specified by a task instruction, a label or
answer space, and a competent baseline system prompt.
\paragraph{Evaluate (GPA dimensions).} Each agent output is scored on four
dimensions by an LLM judge: \emph{target alignment} (did the output address
what was asked?), \emph{reasoning soundness} (are intermediate steps coherent
and grounded?), \emph{execution accuracy} (were format and constraints obeyed?),
and \emph{output integrity} (is the answer correct, complete, non-fabricated?).
The judge is reference-based (it sees the gold answer) and uses a stronger model
than the agent.
\paragraph{Improve (reflective Pareto optimization).} Starting from the
baseline prompt, the optimizer iteratively proposes new prompts by
(i) \emph{reflective mutation} --- inspecting failing training examples and
rewriting the prompt to fix the observed failure mode --- and (ii)
\emph{system-aware merge} of two strong parents. Candidates are scored on
training minibatches; a Pareto front over (objective, GPA) is maintained, and
the best composite candidate is returned. Crucially, \emph{the optimizer never
observes the test split}.

\section{Experimental Design}
\paragraph{Unit of analysis.} One \emph{agent} $=$ one (task, baseline prompt)
pair. We measure each agent twice on the \emph{same held-out test set}: under
its baseline prompt and under the TEI-optimized prompt. The paired contrast
feeds the across-agent statistics ($n=\nAgents$).
\paragraph{Tasks.} The suite spans \nAgents{} industries (Table~\ref{tab:per})
and a difficulty range of 2--20 classes plus two numeric (math) tasks. Roughly
half are drawn from public datasets (e.g., GSM8K~\cite{gsm8k2021}, banking-style
intent detection~\cite{banking77}, news, emotion, toxicity, spam, fine-grained
sentiment); the remainder are authored for industries lacking public data
(insurance, legal, telecom, logistics, real estate, HR, cybersecurity, energy,
agriculture, travel, gaming, government, manufacturing, SaaS) using a
generate-then-validate procedure: a strong model generates label-conditioned
examples, and an independent labeling pass retains only examples whose label is
unanimously recovered. All data is committed.
\paragraph{Models.} Agent under test: \texttt{claude-haiku-4-5}. Judge and
optimizer: \texttt{claude-sonnet-4-5}. Using a stronger, different model to
judge and optimize a weaker agent reduces self-preference bias and reflects a
realistic deployment (cheap agent, capable optimizer).
\paragraph{Endpoints.} The \emph{primary} endpoint is an objective,
code-computed score (label-exact-match accuracy for classification; numeric
exact match for arithmetic). \emph{Secondary} endpoints are the four GPA
dimensions and their mean.
\paragraph{Baselines.} Baseline prompts are competent first drafts that include
the allowed label set; TEI must earn its gains through format discipline, label
disambiguation, and decision rules --- not by revealing information withheld
from the baseline.
\paragraph{Analysis (pre-registered).} Primary test: two-sided paired $t$-test
on per-agent objective deltas. Robustness: Wilcoxon signed-rank. Effect size:
Cohen's $d_z$ with a 10{,}000-sample bootstrap $95\%$ CI. Endpoints are
Holm-corrected~\cite{holm1979}. A pre-registered secondary analysis restricts
to the \emph{headroom subset} (baseline objective $<0.9$), since a saturated
metric cannot improve. Configuration: \texttt{seed}$=0$, 6 optimization
iterations, train/test sizes $15/15$--$20$.

\section{Results}
Table~\ref{tab:agg} reports the aggregate paired analysis.
Figure~\ref{fig:slope} shows every agent's held-out objective score before and
after TEI; Figure~\ref{fig:delta} the distribution of per-agent deltas;
Figure~\ref{fig:dims} the GPA dimensions; and Figure~\ref{fig:head} the
relationship between baseline headroom and improvement. Per-agent results are in
Table~\ref{tab:per}.

\begin{figure}[t]\centering

\caption{TEI improvement vs.\ baseline headroom: gains concentrate where the
baseline leaves room to improve.}
\label{fig:head}
\end{figure}

\paragraph{Primary endpoint.} TEI improves the objective score from \objBase{}
to \objAfter{} on held-out data (mean $\Delta=\objDelta$, $95\%$ CI
$[\objCILow,\objCIHigh]$, $t=\objT$, $p=\objP$, $d_z=\objDz$). Of \nAgents{}
agents, \objWins{} improve, \objTies{} are unchanged (mostly at the ceiling or
on hard binary tasks), and \objLosses{} declines.
\paragraph{Secondary endpoints.} The GPA aggregate rises from \gpaBase{} to
\gpaAfter{} ($\Delta=\gpaDelta$, $p=\gpaP$). Decomposing by dimension
(Table~\ref{tab:agg}), the gains are concentrated in \emph{execution accuracy}
and \emph{target alignment} --- exactly the facets a prompt can control ---
whereas \emph{reasoning soundness} is essentially flat, consistent with the
intuition that prompt edits do not endow the base model with new reasoning
ability.
\paragraph{Headroom.} Restricting to agents whose baseline objective is below
$0.9$ ($n=\headN$), the mean improvement is $\headDelta$ ($p=\headP$,
$d_z=\headDz$), and Figure~\ref{fig:head} shows a clear negative relationship
between baseline score and improvement: TEI helps most precisely where there is
room to help.

\caption{Per-agent held-out results (objective and GPA), sorted by industry.}
\label{tab:per}

\end{table}

\section{Discussion}
The results support a nuanced claim: an evaluation-guided reflective prompt
optimizer reliably improves a weak agent on tasks with structure a prompt can
exploit (label disambiguation, output formatting, decision rules), and does so
in a way that \emph{generalizes} to unseen inputs. It does not manufacture
reasoning ability, and it cannot move already-saturated or capability-bound
tasks. This heterogeneity is a feature of an honest evaluation: aggregate gains
are real and significant, but they are mechanistically explained rather than
uniform.

\section{Limitations and Threats to Validity}
The agent is a single prompt-conditioned LLM call, isolating the prompt as the
manipulated variable; structural code fixes (also part of the full TEI tool)
are out of scope here. Judge and agent share a provider (Anthropic), though not
a model; cross-provider judging would further reduce shared-bias concerns.
Per-task test sets are modest ($\approx$15--20 examples); the unit of
statistical analysis is the agent ($n=\nAgents$), and per-task estimates carry
wider intervals. Authored tasks, though model-validated, are synthetic; the
public-dataset subset corroborates the authored subset. This study
demonstrates improvement \emph{over a fair baseline}; it does not isolate
whether GPA-guided reflection beats undirected prompt search --- a head-to-head
ablation (TEI vs.\ random prompt search vs.\ optimizer-only) is the natural next
step and is supported by the released harness.
Finally, in full disclosure, the classification scorer extracts the predicted
label by its latest mention in the output; a verbose baseline that names other
labels inside its reasoning can thus be mis-extracted, whereas a terse optimized
output is scored correctly. The scorer is fixed a priori and applied identically
to both conditions, so the paired comparison is fair, but part of the objective
gain reflects output-format discipline rather than solely improved
classification --- consistent with execution accuracy being the most-improved
dimension and with the semantically-scored GPA aggregate showing a smaller but
still significant gain. All per-example outputs are released, enabling re-scoring
under any alternative extraction rule.

\section{Reproducibility}
All code, the \nAgents{} task datasets (train/test JSON), per-example output
traces, optimized prompts, the analysis scripts, and a content-addressed
response cache are released. The pre-registered analysis plan is committed
alongside the code. A single command reproduces the full run.

\section{Ethics}
Tasks include content moderation and clinical-style triage framed only as text
classification; outputs are not medical advice and the triage data is
illustrative. Public datasets retain their original licenses, recorded per task.

\section{Conclusion}
Under a conservative, held-out, objective protocol with judge--agent
separation, the TEI loop produces a statistically significant, large
improvement in agent quality across \nAgents{} diverse tasks ($\Delta=\objDelta$
objective, $p=\objP$, $d_z=\objDz$), concentrated where headroom exists and on
the dimensions a prompt controls. Code, data, traces, and prompts are released
at \url{https://github.com/ojavadli/tei-bench}.
